A B S T R A C T Background Motor neuron disease (MND), also known as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), is a progressive neurodegenerative condition that may cause dysphagia, as well as limb weakness, dysarthria, emotional lability, and respiratory failure.
S35: Since normal salivary production is 0.5 L to 1.5 L daily, loss of salivary clearance due to dysphagia leads to salivary pooling and sialorrhea, ofen resulting in distress and inconvenience to people with MND.
S36: This is an update of a review first published in 2011.
S37: Objectives To assess the eﬀects of treatments for sialorrhea in MND, including medications, radiotherapy and surgery.
S38: Search methods On 27 August 2021, we searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register, CENTRAL, MEDLINE, Embase, AMED, CINAHL, ClinicalTrials.gov and the WHO ICTRP.
S39: We checked the bibliographies of the identified randomized trials and contacted trial authors as needed.
S40: We contacted known experts in the field to identify further published and unpublished papers.
S41: Selection criteria We included randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and quasi-RCTs, including cross-over trials, on any intervention for sialorrhea and related symptoms, compared with each other, placebo or no intervention, in people with ALS/MND.
S42: Data collection and analysis We used standard methodological procedures expected by Cochrane.
S43: Main results We identified four RCTs involving 110 participants with MND who were described as having intractable sialorrhea or bulbar dysfunction.
S44: A well-designed study of botulinum toxin B compared to placebo injected into the parotid and submandibular glands of 20 participants showed that botulinum toxin B may produce participant-reported improvement in sialorrhea, but the confidence interval (CI) was also consistent with no eﬀect.
S45: Six of nine participants in the botulinum group and two of nine participants in the placebo group reported improvement (risk ratio (RR) 3.00, 95% CI 0.81 to 11.08; 1 RCT; 18 participants; low-certainty evidence).
S46: An objective measure indicated that botulinum toxin B probably reduced saliva production (in mL/5 min) at eight weeks compared to placebo (MD −0.50, 95% CI −1.07 to 0.07; Treatment for sialorrhea (excessive saliva) in people with motor neuron disease/amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (Review) Copyright © 2022 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S47: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 1 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S50: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 18 participants, moderate-certainty evidence).
S51: Botulinum toxin B may have little to no eﬀect on quality of life, measured on the Schedule for Evaluation of Individual Quality of Life direct weighting scale (SEIQoL-DW; 0-100, higher values indicate better quality of life) (MD −2.50, 95% CI −17.34 to 12.34; 1 RCT; 17 participants; low-certainty evidence).
S52: The rate of adverse events may be similar with botulinum toxin B and placebo (20 participants; low-certainty evidence).
S53: Trialists did not consider any serious events to be related to treatment.
S54: A randomized pilot study of botulinum toxin A or radiotherapy in 20 participants, which was at high risk of bias, provided very low-certainty evidence on the primary outcome of the Drool Rating Scale (DRS; range 8 to 39 points, higher scores indicate worse drooling) at 12 weeks (eﬀect size −4.8, 95% CI −10.59 to 0.92; P = 0.09; 1 RCT; 16 participants).
S55: Quality of life was not measured.
S56: Evidence for adverse events, measured immediately afer treatment (RR 7.00, 95% CI 1.04 to 46.95; 20 participants), and afer four weeks (when two people in each group had viscous saliva) was also very uncertain.
S57: A phase 2, randomized, placebo-controlled cross-over study of 20 mg dextromethorphan hydrobromide and 10 mg quinidine sulfate (DMQ) found that DMQ may produce a participant-reported improvement in sialorrhea, indicated by a slight improvement (decrease) in mean scores for the primary outcome, the Center for Neurologic Study Bulbar Function Scale (CNS-BFS).
S58: Mean total CNS-BFS (range 21 (no symptoms) to 112 (maximum symptoms)) was 53.45 (standard error (SE) 1.07) for the DMQ treatment period and 59.31 (SE 1.10) for the placebo period (mean diﬀerence) MD −5.85, 95% CI -8.77 to -2.93) with a slight decrease in the CNS-BFS sialorrhea subscale score (range 7 (no symptoms) to 35 (maximum symptoms)) compared to placebo (MD –1.52, 95% CI -2.52 to -0.52) (1 RCT; 60 participants; moderate- certainty evidence).
S59: The trial did not report an objective measure of saliva production or measure quality of life.
S60: The study was at an unclear risk of bias.
S61: Adverse events were similar to other trials of DMQ, and may occur at a similar rate as placebo (moderate-certainty evidence, 60 participants), with the most common side eﬀects being constipation, diarrhea, nausea, and dizziness.
S62: Nausea and diarrhea on DMQ treatment resulted in one withdrawal.
S63: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled cross-over study of scopolamine (hyoscine), administered using a skin patch, involved 10 randomized participants, of whom eight provided eﬀicacy data.
S64: The participants were unrepresentative of clinic cohorts under routine clinical care as they had feeding tubes and tracheostomy ventilation, and the study was at high risk of bias.
S65: The trial provided very low- certainty evidence on sialorrhea in the short term (7 days' treatment, measured on the Amyotrophic Lateral Scelerosis Functional Rating Scale-Revised (ALSFRS-R) saliva item (P = 0.572)), and the amount of saliva production in the short term, as indicated by the weight of a cotton roll (P = 0.674), or daily oral suction volume (P = 0.69).
S66: Quality of life was not measured.
S67: Adverse events evidence was also very uncertain.
S68: One person treated with scopolamine had a dry mouth and one died of aspiration pneumonia considered unrelated to treatment.
S69: Authors' conclusions There is some low-certainty or moderate-certainty evidence for the use of botulinum toxin B injections to salivary glands and moderate- certainty evidence for the use of oral dextromethorphan with quinidine (DMQ) for the treatment of sialorrhea in MND.
S70: Evidence on radiotherapy versus botulinum toxin A injections, and scopolamine patches is too uncertain for any conclusions to be drawn.
S71: Further research is required on treatments for sialorrhea.
S72: Data are needed on the problem of sialorrhea in MND and its measurement, both by participant self-report measures and objective tests.
S73: These will allow the development of better RCTs.
S74: P L A I N L A N G U A G E S U M M A R Y Treatment of unintentional drooling of saliva in motor neuron disease Review question To assess the eﬀects of treatments for drooling of saliva in motor neuron disease (MND), which is also known as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
S75: Background Unintentional drooling of saliva (sialorrhea) is a distressing symptom suﬀered by many people with MND because of diﬀiculty swallowing.
S76: The management of sialorrhea currently involves the use of suction, drug treatments and more invasive approaches, including injection of botulinum toxin to the salivary (parotid and submandibular) glands, radiotherapy to the salivary glands, and surgery to tie oﬀ the salivary gland ducts.
S77: This is an update of a review first published in 2011.
S78: Study characteristics Cochrane review authors identified four trials that could be included in the review.
S79: In the first, 20 people with MND received a single injection of botulinum toxin or placebo (dummy treatment) into the salivary glands on each side of the head.
S80: Another study compared botulinum toxin and radiotherapy and was not blinded (which means that neither the participants nor the staﬀ knew whether active or placebo treatment had been administered).

S85: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews uncertainty about the results.
S86: Furthermore, the investigators tested five doses of botulinum and three doses of radiotherapy in a group of 20 people, making it diﬀicult to interpret results.
S87: A third trial was a cross-over study of DMQ, a combination of dextromethorphan hydrobromide and quinidine sulfate.
S88: The 60 people with MND who took part were randomly allocated into two groups; one group received DMQ for about a month, had a drug washout (drug-free period) for about two weeks and then received placebo for about a month; the second group started with placebo for a month, before being switched to DMQ afer the washout period.
S89: The fourth study administered scopolamine (hyoscine) patches or placebo patches to 10 people with severe MND.
S90: A cross-over design was used, with two treatment periods of seven days and a washout period for seven days in between.
S91: Key results and quality of the evidence Data from the first study suggested that botulinum toxin injection may produce an improvement in participant-reported salivation compared to placebo, based on evidence in 20 people, but the results also allow for no eﬀect.
S92: The study appeared to be well designed and well conducted, but it proved diﬀicult to maintain blinding, which leads to uncertainty about measures that rely on a participant's opinion.
S93: Objective tests like measuring saliva volumes are arduous for both patients and staﬀ, but they can provide very useful data; this study provided evidence that botulinum toxin probably reduces the average volume of saliva at week eight afer injection.
S94: Botulinum toxin may have no more side eﬀects than placebo injection and may have little eﬀect on quality of life.
S95: The DMQ trial reported improvements in scores for saliva, speech and swallowing from a 21-item patient-reported outcome measure and improvement in speech but not saliva or swallowing from other patient-reported outcome measures.
S96: It is diﬀicult to say whether the diﬀerences were enough to be important in practice.
S97: Clinicians were more likely to rate participants as having improved bulbar function (comprising saliva, speech and swallowing) if they were in the DMQ treatment phase as opposed to the placebo treatment phase.
S98: The side eﬀects were as expected for DMQ; the most frequent were constipation, diarrhea, nausea, and dizziness; side eﬀects may be no more common with DMQ than with placebo.
S99: The study did not measure quality of life or objective measures of swallowing.
S100: The study appeared to be well conducted but success regarding blinding is unknown.
S101: The evidence from studies of botulinum toxin and radiotherapy and scopolamine (hyoscine) patches was too uncertain for any conclusions to be drawn.
S102: Further research on botulinum toxin, DMQ or even a combination of them would be valuable.
S103: Future trials need to be large enough to detect statistically significant eﬀects and more research is needed on outcome measures.
S104: Although other treatments have been investigated in case-control studies (which generally provide a lower quality of evidence than randomized studies), there was no evidence to compare the eﬀectiveness of other diﬀerent treatments for sialorrhea.
S105: Further research is needed to compare the diﬀerent treatments available and to optimize treatment regimens.
S106: The evidence in the review is current to August 2021.

S196: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews B A C K G R O U N D Description of the condition Motor neuron disease (MND), also known as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by relatively selective death of upper motor neurons in the cerebral cortex and lower motor neurons in the brainstem and spinal cord (Shaw 1999).
S197: This gives rise to a combination of upper and lower motor neuron signs and symptoms, such as weakness in the limb and bulbar muscles with atrophy, spasticity and weight loss.
S198: The onset of MND can be insidious.
S199: By the time a diagnosis is made, the person may have significant functional deficits and symptom control needs.
S200: Most people die within two to five years afer diagnosis, usually from respiratory failure as a result of ventilatory muscle weakness (Rowland 2001).
S201: The incidence of ALS/MND is 1 to 2.6 per 100,000 per year (Talbott 2016).
S202: At any one time, there are approximately 4000 aﬀected people in the United Kingdom (NICE 2016), and 25,000 in North America (McGuire 1996).
S203: In MND, approximately 70% of people present with limb involvement and 30% with bulbar symptoms such dysphagia, dysarthria, hoarseness and hypophonia (Abhinav 2007).
S204: Eighty per cent of people with MND will develop bulbar weakness at some stage of the disease (Oliver 1996).
S205: Many people benefit from alternative feeding methods such as percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) or radiologically inserted gastrostomy (RIG/ PRG) (Leigh 2003; Mitsomoto 2003).
S206: Sialorrhea refers to the unintentional loss of saliva from the mouth.
S207: For people with MND, sialorrhea is not caused by increased production of saliva but by the inability to swallow secretions because of tongue spasticity, weakness of face, mouth and pharyngeal muscles, and loss of oropharyngeal co-ordination and function (Veis 2000).
S208: The issue of excess saliva is not rare among people with MND, of whom 28% report slight excess saliva, 17% moderate-marked excess saliva and 4% marked drooling (Bradley 2001).
S209: About half of people with MND who experience sialorrhea describe themselves as bothered by the symptom (Schlüter 2018).
S210: There are two main types of saliva produced by the three major salivary glands: serous saliva, which is thin and watery, and mucoidal saliva, which is thicker and more viscous.
S211: The nose and lungs also contribute to the flow of secretions, usually in the form of thick, tenacious 'phlegm' (Newall 1996).
S212: All these forms of secretions can cause people discomfort.
S213: Serous saliva can be watery and constantly dribbling from the side of the mouth, and mucoidal saliva may be tenacious and thick, making it diﬀicult to clear and contributing to the sensation of choking and associated panic.
S214: The skin may become excoriated from constant exposure to moisture, voice quality may be compromised and sleep may be disturbed by secretion build-up.
S215: These symptoms may lead to increased fatigue, embarrassment, social withdrawal and isolation.
S216: Therefore, sialorrhea can significantly aﬀect quality of life.
S217: Riluzole, a putative glutamate release blocker, and edaravone, a free radical scavenger, are currently the only drugs licensed for human use in MND.
S218: Their eﬀects are modest; riluzole prolongs survival by about two to three months in people with MND (Miller 2007) and edaravone reports slowing of disability progression in early, mildly aﬀected patients with forced vital capacity at least 80% predicted (Edaravone Study Group 2017).
S219: Therefore, symptom control and maximization of quality of life are the principal aims of any treatment (Oliver 1996).
S220: Description of the intervention At present, the treatment of sialorrhea is largely a matter of trial and error, using a variety of drug treatments, natural remedies, oral suction, and more invasive approaches, such as injection of botulinum toxin into salivary glands, radiotherapy to salivary glands or ligation of salivary gland ducts.
S221: Behavioral techniques, such as purposeful swallowing of the saliva as it builds up in the mouth and before speaking, are also advocated in the early management stages.
S222: This has been shown to be eﬀective in people with Parkinson's disease where a metronome brooch was used as a reminder to cue a conscious saliva swallow (Marks 2001).
S223: Management appears largely governed by the personal preference of the clinician and, in the case of more invasive measures, the local availability of these techniques.
S224: Frequently, the person with MND will need to try a number of treatment options with varying degrees of success.
S225: Several potential treatments have been described in the literature.
S226: Salivation is mainly mediated through parasympathetic stimulation, with the neurotransmitter acetylcholine binding at muscarinic receptors in the salivary glands (Meningaud 2006).
S227: Drugs with cholinergic muscarinic receptor antagonist properties, such as atropine, scopolamine (hyoscine), glycopyrronium bromide and tricyclic antidepressants can be used to treat sialorrhea in MND (Borasio 1997).
S228: Whereas the stimulation of cholinergic receptors produces thin, serous secretions, that of beta adrenergic receptors produces thick protein and mucus-rich secretions.
S229: Beta blockers have been shown to produce relief from thick secretions in 12 of 16 people with bulbar MND (Newall 1996).
S230: The mucolytic drug, carbocisteine may, also be beneficial in MND, as it reduces elastic modules of mucus and improves mucociliary transport (Sakakura 1985).
S231: Use of botulinum toxin to reduce salivary flow in MND was first hypothesized in modern day literature by Bushara in 1997 (Bushara 1997).
S232: It is thought that botulinum toxin injections reduce sialorrhea by blocking the release of acetylcholine at the cholinergic neurosecretory junction of salivary glands (Burgen 1949).
S233: There are now several series reported, utilizing diﬀerent preparations of botulinum toxin and treatment regimens, administered by direct injection or a transductal approach, with varying outcome measures (Contarino 2007; Costa 2008; Giess 2000; Guidubaldi 2011; Jackson 2009; Lipp 2003; Manrique 2005; Paracka 2019; Petracca 2015; Porta 2001; Scott 2005; Verma 2006).
S234: Recently, dextromethorphan with quinidine sulfate has been investigated as a potential treatment for sialorrhea; the interest arose from patients with MND reporting improvement in speech, swallowing and excess secretions following prescription of dextromethorphan for pseudobulbar aﬀect.
S235: While such unblinded patient reports must be treated with caution, it has been suggested that pseudobulbar aﬀect may be related to damage to corticobulbar pathways (Wilson 1924), which would also be involved in other bulbar functions such as swallowing of secretions.
S236: In addition, various destructive measures have been trialled in open-label studies as a means to reduce salivary production.
S237: One approach is radiotherapy to the salivary glands, either once or in divided fractions (Andersen 2001; Bourry 2013; Guy 2011; Harriman Treatment for sialorrhea (excessive saliva) in people with motor neuron disease/amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (Review) Copyright © 2022 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S238: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 10 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S241: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 2001; Kasarskis 2011; Neppelberg 2007; Stalpers 2002; Winterholler 2001).
S242: Another area is surgical interventions, to excise salivary glands, or destroy nerve supply to the gland by neurectomy of the tympanic nerve (Janzen 1988).
S243: Why it is important to do this review This Cochrane Systematic Review notes that several treatments have been described in case series and open-label studies, utilizing a variety of outcome measures and treating a range of case severity.
S244: A systematic review may benefit clinicians and inform better practice.
S245: This is an update of a review first published in 2011.
S246: O B J E C T I V E S To assess the eﬀects of treatments for sialorrhea in motor neuron disease, including medications, radiotherapy and surgery.
S247: M E T H O D S Criteria for considering studies for this review Types of studies We considered all randomized and quasi-randomized controlled studies of the treatment of sialorrhea in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease (ALS/MND) for inclusion in the review.
S248: Quasi-randomized trials allocate participants to treatments based on methods that are partly systematic, for example, based on case record number or day of arrival.
S249: We included such trials because there is little research on this topic.
S251: The nature of treatment interventions made it impossible to include only double-blinded trials, and so non-blinded trials were eligible.
S252: We included trials that were neither randomized nor quasi-randomized in the Discussion.
S253: We planned to include studies irrespective of their publication status and language of publication.
S254: Types of participants We only included for review trials that explicitly stated that participants had been diagnosed with probable or definite MND or ALS according to recognized criteria, preferably the El Escorial Criteria (Brooks 2000).
S255: Participants were over 18 years of age and of either sex.
S256: We would have included data from studies of sialorrhea of mixed etiologies providing it was possible to identify data on MND separately.
S257: Types of interventions We considered the following interventions compared with each other, placebo or no intervention: 1.
S258: Any drug treatment administered via any route.
S260: Injection of botulinum toxin in parotid or submandibular glands, or both.
S264: Surgical techniques, for example, the ligation of parotid and submandibular salivary ducts.
S266: Other treatments identified in the literature, for example, complementary therapies.
S267: Types of outcome measures Primary outcomes 1.
S268: Participant-reported improvement in sialorrhea reported in the short term (one to 12 weeks) and long term (more than 12 weeks).
S269: Participant-reported improvement was the change from baseline measured using a validated scale, or by asking participants if they improved or not.
S271: Reduction in the amount of saliva production using an objective measure, measured in the short term (one to 12 weeks) and long term (more than 12 weeks), using, for example, the weight of swabs or amount of tissue used pre- and post- intervention and reported as the change from baseline.
S273: Quality of life of participants, measured by a validated scale in the short term (one to 12 weeks) and long term (more than 12 weeks).
S275: Adverse eﬀects attributable to any intervention, summarized as any adverse events, severe adverse events, or adverse events leading to cessation of treatment.
S276: Search methods for identification of studies Electronic searches We searched the following databases on 9 May 2019, 29 September 2020, and 27 August 2021: • Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register via the Cochrane Register of Studies (CRS-Web; until search date; Appendix 1); • Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) via the Cochrane Library via CRS-Web (until search date; Appendix 2); • MEDLINE via Ovid SP (1946 to 26 August 2021; Appendix 3); • Embase via Ovid SP (1974 to 2021 Week 33; Appendix 4); • AMED via Ovid SP (Allied and Complementary Medicine; 1985 to August 2021; Appendix 1); • CINAHL via EBSCOhost (Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature; 1937 to 27 August 2021; Appendix 2); • ClinicalTrials.gov (until search date; Appendix 3); • WHO International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (ICTRP; until search date; Appendix 4).
S277: In this search, we did not apply any limitations to date, language, document type, or publication status.
S278: Searching other resources We reviewed all bibliographies of the identified randomized trials and contacted trial authors as needed.
S279: We contacted known experts in the field and pharmaceutical companies to identify further published and unpublished papers.
S280: We included papers in languages other than English and translated them as necessary.
S281: We would have examined any retractions and errata.
S282: Data collection and analysis Selection of studies All review authors independently checked titles and abstracts identified from the searches, to produce short-lists of potentially relevant studies.

S295: various participant-, carer- or clinician-assessed scales), or various objective measures, we collected data for all outcomes and time points.
S296: For papers requiring translation, we would have either used a data extraction performed by the translator or two authors would have independently completed a data extraction form from a translation of the study.
S297: If the translator had extracted data, we would have checked numerical data against the paper if possible.
S298: Assessment of risk of bias in included studies Two review authors (CAY and CE) independently assessed risk of bias according to the methods in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins 2011).
S299: The assessment took into account sequence generation, allocation concealment, blinding (for participant-reported outcomes, objective outcomes, clinician-reported outcomes, carer-reported outcomes), whether incomplete outcome data was addressed, selective outcome reporting (including incomplete outcome definition) and other sources of bias.
S300: We considered sources of bias specific to cross-over trials, such as carry-over or period eﬀects under 'Other sources of bias'.
S301: We considered baseline imbalance inconsistent with chance in relation to randomization and allocation concealment rather than 'other sources of bias'.
S302: We graded these items as at high, low, or unclear risk of bias.
S303: We used unclear when the risk of bias was uncertain or the item was not relevant to the study.
S304: We specified that if agreement was poor, we would reassess the studies and reach agreement by consensus.
S305: We described the risk of bias of included studies, and discussed the overall reliability of the evidence in the light of this.
S306: Had data been appropriate for this, we would have undertaken sensitivity analyses to demonstrate the eﬀect of excluding studies with an unclear or high risk of bias in any domain from the analysis.
S307: We made summary assessments of bias, judging studies as being at low overall risk of bias if the judgement was low risk for all key domains.
S308: An unclear risk of bias in a key domain rendered a study as being at unclear overall risk of bias, and a high-risk judgement in a key domain resulted in a high overall risk of bias for the study (Higgins 2011).
S309: Measures of treatment eﬀect We reported all the primary and secondary outcomes under consideration.
S310: We expressed results as risk ratios (RRs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for dichotomous outcomes (participant- reported improvement in sialorrhea, if reported, as improved or not, and adverse events), and mean diﬀerences (MDs) and 95% CIs for continuous outcomes (participant-reported improvement in sialorrhea, or reduction in amount of saliva, quality of life).
S311: We would have combined data using a standardized mean diﬀerence (SMD) with 95% CI if trials using diﬀerent scales to measure the same outcome could be pooled (ensuring a consistent direction of eﬀect).
S312: We would have presented rules of thumb interpretation of eﬀect sizes (0.2 represents a small eﬀect, 0.5 a moderate eﬀect, and 0.8 a large eﬀect) alongside the results (Schünemann 2022a).
S313: We would have used a generic inverse variance analysis to pool results from cross-over studies and parallel-group studies (Deeks 2022).
S314: Economic issues We planned to discuss the cost-eﬀectiveness of interventions in the Discussion, where there was information available to do this.
S315: Unit of analysis issues If it had been possible to combine two comparisons from the same multi-arm trial (e.g.
S316: drug A versus placebo and drug B versus placebo) in the same meta-analysis, we would have combined intervention groups to create a single pairwise comparison to avoid double-counting participants, as recommended in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions.
S317: If this approach had not been clinically appropriate, we would have halved the 'shared' group (Higgins 2022).
S318: We included results from correctly analyzed randomized cross-over trials if the design included an appropriate washout period and the treatment period was short (ALS is a rapidly progressive condition).
S319: We would otherwise have reported first period data, if data allowed.
S320: We considered potential biases arising from a cross-over design under 'Other sources of bias'.

S338: Where data were available, we presented the following outcomes, reported at eight weeks (or according to protocol if eight-week data were not provided).
S339: • Participant-reported improvement in sialorrhea; • Reduction in the amount of saliva production using an objective measure: change in volume of saliva (measured in mL saliva produced per five minutes); • Change in quality of life (using outcome measure reported by study); • Adverse events (any).
S340: When sialorrhoea was measured in multiple ways, we preferred specific drooling scales or the ALSFRS salivation subscale in preference to visual analog scales in Summary of findings tables.
S341: Where several objective measures of saliva volume were used, we reported them all.

S343: When data were not available to calculate mean changes from baseline, where no major baseline imbalance was present, we reported the diﬀerences between groups in values at the given time point.
S344: We used the five GRADE considerations (study limitations, consistency of eﬀect, imprecision, indirectness and publication bias) to assess the quality of a body of evidence (studies that contributed data for the prespecified outcomes).
S345: We used methods and recommendations described in Chapter 14 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Schünemann 2022b) using GRADEpro Guideline Development Tool sofware (GRADEpro GDT 2019).

S347: We justified decisions to downgrade or upgrade the quality of studies using footnotes, and we made comments to aid readers' understanding of the review, where necessary.

S365: (Continued) Included studies We identified four randomized controlled trials (RCTs) in motor neuron disease (MND) (Jackson 2009; Odachi 2017; Smith 2017; Weikamp 2016).
S367: All four studies included participants with probable or definite amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) according to World Federation of Neurology (WFN) criteria.
S369: Three trials were placebo-controlled, investigating botulinum toxin, a combination of dextromethorphan and quinidine sulfate (DMQ), and scopolamine patch (Jackson 2009; Odachi 2017; Smith 2017).
S370: The fourth trial compared radiotherapy with botulinum toxin (Weikamp 2016).
S371: Botulinum toxin type B versus placebo Participants In Jackson 2009, participants had probable or definite ALS according to WFN criteria and had to have sialorrhea refractory to treatment with at least two anticholinergic medications (or be intolerant of anticholinergic treatments).
S372: Participants were aged 21 to 85 years, had forced vital capacity > 20% predicted, could not have received any previous treatment with botulinum toxin, and were recruited from three sites.
S373: Interventions and comparators The study by Jackson and colleagues randomized 20 people with MND and sialorrhea refractory to medical therapy to either botulinum toxin type B to parotid and submandibular glands, or placebo (Jackson 2009).
S374: Two injections were given to each of the parotid and submandibular glands bilaterally, that is, eight injections per participant.
S375: The injection, via a monopolar electromyography needle attached to a 1 mL syringe, contained either 1 mL normal saline or botulinum toxin type B 2500 units diluted with normal saline to a total volume of 1 mL.
S376: Absence of motor unit activity was used to confirm needle placement in glandular/sof tissue.
S377: Each parotid gland received 0.1 mL at two sites, directing the needle between the sternocleidomastoid muscle and angle of mandible.
S378: Each submandibular gland received two injections of 0.15 mL, placing the needle in the submandibular triangle.
S379: Pretreatment with topical lidocaine/prilocaine was available if requested prior to the injection.
S380: Following the 12-week double-blind phase, participants could enrol into an open-label extension for three months.
S381: This used the same method of injection, but the dose of botulinum toxin could be adjusted so that the total dose did not exceed 5000 units.
S382: Outcomes Participants were assessed at baseline which was the day of randomization and injection under electromyographic guidance, and then at 2, 4, 8 and 12 weeks, with a telephone follow-up at one week which could trigger an attendance if the Revised ALS Functional Rating Scale (ALSFRS-R; Cedarbaum 1999) swallowing or respiratory scale had changed by more than one point.
S383: The primary outcome was the participant's global impression of change recorded on a five-point Likert scale eight weeks afer injection, subsequently dichotomized into "improvement" or "no improvement".
S384: The choice of eight weeks afer injection appears a legitimate choice, given the temporary nature of botulinum treatment and the clinical imperative that a participant would have a reasonable period of symptom benefit following invasive treatment.
S385: Secondary outcome measures were listed as change in saliva volumes (mL/5 min), number of times suctioned, participant and caregiver assessments of benefit as visual analogue scales (VAS), ALSFRS-R, and quality of life measured using the Schedule for Evaluation of Individual Quality of Life direct weighting (SEIQoL- DW) (Hickey 1996), a quality of life self-rating scale using five cues chosen and rated by the participant.
S386: Concomitant treatment with anticholinergic drugs was kept stable or allowed to reduce during the trial and outcome measures were collected at the same time of day and afer the participants had refrained from eating or drinking for one hour.
S387: Questionnaires were done independently by participant and caregiver and completed before any interaction with the study physicians.
S388: Radiotherapy versus botulinum toxin type A Participants The study by Weikamp and colleagues randomized 20 people with MND and "physically and socially disabling drooling" refractory to treatment, into a randomized, parallel-group study of either botulinum toxin type A or radiotherapy to salivary glands (Weikamp 2016).
S389: All participants had probable or definite ALS as defined by the El Escorial criteria and could not have dementia, malnutrition, dehydration or other neurological diseases.
S390: Interventions and comparators Participants were phoned two weeks afer treatment and if treatment had not been eﬀective and there had been no adverse eﬀects, they were able to have a repeat treatment at week four.
S391: Treatment was either botulinum toxin A (20 MU or 50 MU) injected into both parotid glands under ultrasound guidance or a single fraction of 7.0 Gy radiotherapy to parotid glands (excluding the cranial part 1 cm below the zygomatic arch) and the posterior part of the submandibular glands.

S396: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews A was 50 MU into each gland, unless electromyography indicated lower motor neuron involvement of oropharyngeal muscles, in which case the dose was reduced to 20 MU into each gland.
S397: Outcomes Participants were randomized to two equal groups and were assessed before treatment, and at weeks 4 and 12 afer treatment; the assessments at weeks 0 and 4 were in clinic and at week 12 were returned by post.
S398: The primary outcome was the Drool Rating Scale (DRS), a self-report questionnaire of eight items with 4-5 responses per item.
S399: This was designed for children with cerebral palsy and has a score range of 8 to 39, with higher scores indicating worse drooling (Suskind 2002).
S400: Secondary outcomes were: the impression of saliva volume in the mouth (0 to 100 VAS with higher scores indicating too much saliva), global change of drooling afer treatment (1 = markedly worse to 7 = markedly improved), and level of satisfaction with the treatment (1 = markedly dissatisfied to 6 = markedly satisfied).
S401: Attempts to measure saliva volume by counting number of paper handkerchiefs used were deemed unreliable, as some patients used the same one repeatedly and some adopted single use, so this measure was abandoned.
S402: Assessments were conducted on the day of treatment prior to its administration, and 4 and 12 weeks afer treatment.
S403: However, participants were contacted by phone two weeks afer treatment and the protocol allowed re- treatment at four weeks if the participant felt that treatment had not been eﬀective and reported no adverse eﬀects.
S404: Dextromethorphan and quinidine sulfate combination (DMQ) versus placebo Participants All participants in Smith 2017 had probable or definite ALS as defined by the revised WFN criteria, disease duration < two years from time of diagnosis, slow vital capacity at least 50% of normal, and patients taking medication(s) to control salivation had to be on a stable dose for at least 30 days.
S405: They all had bulbar dysfunction manifested by dysarthria and/or dysphagia as determined by the site principal investigator, 'guided' by a Center for Neurologic Study Bulbar Function Scale (CNS-BFS) score ranging from 50 to 80.
S406: Exclusion criteria included prior use of DMQ; current use of dextromethorphan, quinidine, quinine, mefloquine, opioids, or a known sensitivity to those drugs; a history of prolonged QT interval, congenital long QT syndrome, complete atrioventricular block, or concomitant use of drugs that both prolong QT interval and are metabolized by CYP2D6; use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors; invasive ventilator dependence; use of a feeding tube; treatment with botulinum toxin within 90 days of screening, or radiation for control of sialorrhea within 180 days.
S407: Participants were recruited from seven sites.
S408: Sixty people with ALS/MND of less than two years duration and bulbar dysfunction manifested by dysarthria and/or dysphagia were recruited.
S409: Interventions and comparators The study by Smith and colleagues was a phase 2, randomized, placebo-controlled cross-over study examining a combination of 20 mg dextromethorphan hydrobromide and 10 mg quinidine sulfate, marketed as a single capsule (DMQ) (Smith 2017).
S410: The inclusion of quinidine in the combination agent DMQ aims to slow the metabolism of dextromethorphan by P450 2D6 enzymes in the liver (Rosen 2008).
S411: They were randomized to commence either DMQ, or identical placebo, for 28 +/-3 days, before crossing over to the alternate agent, with a 10- to 15-day intervening washout period.
S412: At onset of each treatment period, patients took a single capsule for seven days, and then two capsules 12 hours apart for the remainder of the 28-day period.
S413: Following the second treatment phase, participants had a 28 +/-5-day final washout period before a telephone call terminating the study.
S414: Outcomes Participants were assessed at the beginning and end of each treatment arm.
S415: Assessments included both self-report and rater- reported measures.
S416: The primary outcome was the 21-item CNS- BFS.
S417: This has a score range of 21 to 112, with higher scores indicating worse bulbar function; participants were anticipated to have a score range of 50 to 80 for eligibility.
S418: The CNS-BFS has a 7-item subscale on salivation, with a range of 7 to 35.
S419: Secondary outcomes included changes in the patient-reported VAS for bulbar function and the rater-administered ALSFRS-R. Scopalamine patch versus placebo patch Participants The study by Odachi and colleagues was a randomized, cross- over study comparing a scopolamine patch to a placebo patch (Odachi 2017).
S420: Ten patients diagnosed according to the El Escorial criteria were randomized to either scopolamine (hyoscine) or placebo patches.
S421: Eligibility criteria were made more restrictive afer the study had started and the original power calculation of 20 participants consequently not adhered to.
S422: All participants had ALS as defined by the El Escorial criteria.
S423: None had received any medication for sialorrhea before.
S424: Eligibility criteria were altered during the study to exclude people with rapid progression rate (calculated as 48-ALSFRS-R at time of diagnosis/duration [months] from onset to diagnosis ≥ 0.67) and those with marked respiratory hypofunction (%vital capacity ≤ 30%, or PaCO2 ≥ 45 mmH2O) unless ventilation was planned.
S425: The participants were extremely disabled: all had gastrostomy feeding tubes and nine (out of 10) had tracheostomy ventilation.
S426: The cohort was therefore most unrepresentative of standard clinic populations.
S427: The one participant who was able to breathe unassisted had a ALSFRS-R total score of 24/48, the remaining nine had median ALSFRS-R total scores of 4.
S428: Outcome data were available for eight participants.
S429: Interventions and comparators The study compared scopolamine patches against placebo (Odachi 2017).
S430: The dose of scopolamine in the commercially-sourced transdermal patch is not stated.
S431: Patches were applied for seven days, followed by a washout period of seven days, before crossing over to the alternate agent for seven days.
S432: Outcomes Assessments were made using both self-report and rater-reported measures.
S433: The primary outcome was changes in sialorrhea measured using two 10 cm VAS, one for severity and one for diﬀiculty, anchors unstated.
S434: Secondary outcomes included the single item on saliva from the ALSFRS-R, scored 0 to 4.
S435: Saliva volume was measured by increase in weight of a cotton roll placed in the mouth near the second molar area for 5 minutes, and volume of daily oral suction.

S442: Excluded studies The majority of studies identified did not meet eligibility criteria as RCTs or quasi-RCTs in people with MND: • three studies did not provide separate data for participants with MND: ◦one randomized placebo-controlled study of three diﬀerent doses of botulinum toxin or placebo, using an unspecified method, in which it was not possible to separate the data on the 12 people with ALS/MND from the 20 other participants suﬀering from Parkinson's disease, multiple system atrophy or corticobasal degeneration (Lipp 2003); ◦one randomized cross-over study of botulinum toxin A and botulinum toxin B in people with MND and Parkinson's disease.

S622: D I S C U S S I O N Summary of main results An extensive literature review identified just four randomized controlled trials (RCTs) of treatment of sialorrhea in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease (ALS/MND) involving 110 participants.
S623: Jackson and colleagues conducted a well-designed study of a single session of botulinum toxin B injections to parotid and submandibular glands, which found that there may be both participant-reported and objective benefits in participants for some weeks afer the injection, although CIs were also consistent with no eﬀect (Jackson 2009).
S624: Additional information from the study authors enabled confirmation of the reduction in volume of saliva for participants treated with botulinum toxin at two and four weeks afer injection.
S625: This objective measure is important, as blinding proved diﬀicult to maintain, potentially biasing the patient- reported measures.
S626: The primary outcome was participant-reported and was specified as whether the participant had improvement or no improvement in sialorrhea at week eight.
S627: Participant-reported Improvement in sialorrhea favoured botulinum toxin at weeks two and four but not weeks eight or 12.
S628: However, additional information from the study authors allowed calculation of risk ratios (RRs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for improvement or not.
S629: These RRs consistently favoured botulinum toxin at weeks two, four, eight and 12 but showed wide CIs, covering no eﬀect to benefit.
S630: Weikamp and colleagues compared radiotherapy using an initial dose of 7.0 Gy directed at each parotid against 20 or 50 MU botulinum toxin A under ultrasound guidance to parotids only (Weikamp 2016).
S631: The participants were called afer two weeks and if treatment had not been eﬀective and there were no adverse eﬀects, they could have another course of their allocated treatment of radiotherapy or botulinum toxin afer four weeks, providing they remained eligible (one person had three doses of radiotherapy).
S632: Total treatment doses ranged for botulinum toxin from 20 MU to 100 MU in each gland and radiotherapy between 7 Gy to 21 Gy for each gland.
S633: The evidence from this trial was too uncertain for reliable conclusions.
S634: This study was unblinded and patient-reported outcomes may be subject to bias; the authors attempted a count of usage of paper handkerchiefs but found the measure unreliable in clinical practice due to some patients repeatedly using the same handkerchief and others discarding it with each use.
S635: The Drool Rating Scale (DRS) was developed for children with cerebral palsy and does not appear to have been validated in ALS/MND.
S636: Smith and colleagues carried out a phase 2, randomized, placebo- controlled, cross-over study of 20 mg dextromethorphan given Treatment for sialorrhea (excessive saliva) in people with motor neuron disease/amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (Review) Copyright © 2022 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S637: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 22 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S640: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews orally in a combination capsule with 10 mg quinidine sulfate, now marketed as DMQ (Smith 2017).
S641: This reported statistically significant improvements in the primary outcome of self-reported bulbar function using the Center for Neurologic Study Bulbar Function Scale (CNS-BFS).
S642: Some of the secondary outcomes corroborated the primary, such as rater-assessed bulbar function, using the ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised (ALSFRS-R) bulbar subscale.
S643: However, some measures were not convergent; the ALSFRS-R bulbar subscale has one item for each of speech, sialorrhea and swallowing, and the patient-completed visual analogue scale (VAS ) for speech improved on DMQ but those for sialorrhea or swallowing did not.
S644: There was no information provided on the success of maintaining blinding but adverse events may have led to some unblinding.
S645: Odachi and colleagues conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, cross-over study of scopolamine (hyoscine) as the first treatment attempted for people with MND who were all gastrostomy-fed and mostly receiving tracheostomy ventilation (Odachi 2017).
S646: The evidence was too uncertain for any conclusions.
S647: Overall completeness and applicability of evidence There continue to be few RCTs on the treatment of sialorrhea in MND.
S648: There are single randomized studies on each of botulinum toxin, radiotherapy, and DMQ.
S649: Only one study assessed quality of life and only one reported objective measurement of saliva production.
S650: The single study on scopolamine was conducted in a small sample who were not representative of routine clinic populations.
S651: Many treatments which are being used in clinical practice have no randomized trial evidence; further research is required.
S652: Because of the paucity of evidence in this review, we could summarise all comparisons in Summary of findings tables.
S653: In future updates if new evidence emerges, we will prioritise interventions in wide clinical use and comparisons with placebo, as the certainty of evidence for any intervention is no better than moderate.
S654: The inclusion of patient-reported outcome measures remains mandatory in order to explore the patient experience and to examine the clinical significance of any treatment eﬀect.
S655: The methodological quality of using such measures needs improvement.
S656: A patient-reported measure must be validated in that patient group.
S657: Ordinal data from patient- or clinician-reported measures, such as ALSFRS-R, should be analyzed with suitable statistical tests and not using parametric statistics.
S658: More work is needed on the psychometric properties of VAS in MND; pain VAS have been shown not to be linear and their responsiveness varies across the scale (Kersten 2014).
S659: VAS results may be concentrated at extremes of the scale and require ordinal analysis (Heller 2016).
S660: At minimum, authors should describe the anchor statements used to define the upper and lower boundaries of the VAS for the participant completing it.
S661: Finally, for all measures, it is desirable to provide data to indicate whether any change is of clinical, as well as statistical, significance.
S662: While adaptive trial designs may be highly useful in MND research, they need to be designed and powered as adaptive designs from inception.
S663: In one study designed as a parallel comparator study, several regimens were tested as a corollary of providing additional treatments to patients who reported no benefit (Weikamp 2016).
S664: While motivated by commendable desires to be reactive to patient wishes if the patient felt the treatment had failed, this impacted the results profoundly as this study of 20 patients ended up testing eight diﬀerent regimens, and was highly likely underpowered for each of them.
S665: Investigators and patients may need to accept that the consequences of equipoise should include that the treatment does not work so, if it is felt to have failed, this provides useful data for that dose and route of administration and should not prompt testing a diﬀerent dose ad hoc in the same trial.
S666: Careful consideration of trial design for future studies will be important.
S667: Ethical considerations suggest that the placebo- controlled phase should be as short as the postulated mechanism of action of the treatment allows.
S668: Volunteers who commence on the placebo phase might also be automatically oﬀered the active intervention unless safety considerations have arisen in the first phase (delayed commencement design).
S669: Trials will have greater scientific utility if all participants remain blinded, but this is more diﬀicult with such a design where participants will know they are all on active agent by the second half of the study.
S670: With blinding maintained, the participants on active treatment throughout contribute data on long-term safety and sustained response, and the participants who switch to active provide more information on eﬀicacy.
S671: Trials are likely to need multicenter involvement, and the randomization may include comparing more than one dose against placebo, given the lack of preliminary data.
S672: Quality of the evidence In the study by Jackson and colleagues, the eligibility criteria specified that the study population had to have inadequate response to, or intolerance of, anticholinergic medications, and concomitant drug treatment had to be stable or reducing during follow-up (Jackson 2009).
S673: Randomization and analysis appeared good, and data completeness was satisfactory.
S674: In the study by Smith and colleagues, the study population had to be on stable treatments for sialorrhea and preliminary work was done on the criterion validity of the primary outcome measure (Smith 2017).
S675: In both studies, the intervention was described with suﬀicient detail to allow replication by other clinicians.
S676: Despite excellent methodology employed in the botulinum toxin study to maintain blinding, the majority of participants and investigators correctly guessed their treatment allocation at the end of the double-blind phase.
S677: No data were provided on maintenance of blinding in the DMQ study, but some unblinding appeared likely.
S678: These studies showed two sources of unblinding which would be diﬀicult to exclude in future work.
S679: The first is loss of blinding as a consequence of investigating any treatment with some level of clinical benefit, even if temporary.
S680: The clinical context is that people with MND almost always show an inexorably progressive course and, thus, sialorrhea does not spontaneously remit but instead would be anticipated to worsen as bulbar function deteriorates.
S681: The second cause of loss of blinding is adverse events.
S682: Therefore, drug eﬀects are a major source of unbinding in a controlled trial.
S683: When choosing primary outcome measures, it may therefore be preferable to utilize objective quantitative measures less influenced by unblinding.
S684: The most common objective measures assessed volume of saliva produced in unit time.
S685: The study of scopolamine (hyoscine) was able to estimate volume of saliva by measuring increase in weight of cotton roll placed near the second molar in the mouth for five minutes, and by counting volume of daily suction (Odachi 2017).
S686: Change in cotton roll weight has been used in several non- randomized studies (Contarino 2007; Costa 2008; Gilio 2010) and Treatment for sialorrhea (excessive saliva) in people with motor neuron disease/amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (Review) Copyright © 2022 The Cochrane Collaboration.

S690: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews the study by Odachi and colleagues in a severely disabled cohort suggests it may be applicable in most trials (Odachi 2017).
S691: The pilot study of botulinum toxin compared to radiotherapy found that the quantitative measure of number of paper handkerchiefs used was unreliable, providing useful information for other researchers (Weikamp 2016).
S692: Potential biases in the review process The review authors were unaware of biases in the review process other than the possibility that there were studies which had not been reported in the literature.
S693: These might include negative studies which had not been submitted or whose authors had not written a full study report.
S694: There could also be studies not accepted for publication due to methodological issues.
S695: One way to seek such studies is by comparison of the trials registries with publications.
S696: We identified one study commenced in 2012 of bilateral injection with botulinum toxin type A in parotids and submandibular glands (NCT01551940 2012 (EUCTR2010-023161-21-FR)).
S697: As of January 2020, this was unpublished and the PI has not responded to our enquiry.
S698: This study is summarized under ongoing studies.
S699: Agreements and disagreements with other studies or reviews Evidence from non-randomized studies Botulinum toxin There are non-randomized trials, typically open-label, on the use of botulinum toxin to reduce saliva production in MND.
S700: These supported a beneficial eﬀect of botulinum toxin, some utilizing objective, quantitative outcome measures.
S701: Gilio and colleagues injected botulinum toxin bilaterally into parotid glands of 26 people with MND and assessed severity of sialorrhea and amount of disability caused by sialorrhea using VAS and cotton roll weight at two weeks afer injection (Gilio 2010).
S702: The severity of sialorrhea improved and the disabling symptoms diminished, and also cotton roll weight decreased, suggesting less saliva production (Gilio 2010).
S703: Less daily tissue use was also observed in five out of six participants afer botulinum toxin injections to parotids, but only one reported improved quality of life using a single-item scale derived from the McGill Quality of Life Questionnaire (Scott 2005).
S704: In an open-label study of 12 participants with MND and three with Parkinson's disease, Møller and colleagues injected both parotid and submandibular glands with botulinum toxin.
S705: Five of the MND participants dropped out before post-treatment evaluation (Møller 2011).
S706: Giess and colleagues injected botulinum toxin in the parotid glands of only three MND participants and in the parotid and submandibular glands of two MND participants (Giess 2000).
S707: A significant reduction in the number of paper handkerchiefs used daily was found four weeks afer the last injection.
S708: Four of the five participants reported improvement in quality of life associated with marked reduction of sialorrhea, evidenced by reduced radiotracer uptake on parotid salivary gland scintigraphy afer the botulinum injections.
S709: One participant with rapidly progressive disease did not show clinical benefit afer repeated parotid and submandibular gland injections, despite a markedly reduced radiotracer uptake in the parotid glands.
S710: In another open-label study, botulinum toxin was injected into the parotid glands of 10 participants with MND (Verma 2006).
S711: Of the eight people who completed the study, five showed improvement of 2.5 or more on the visual analogue scale (VAS).
S712: In addition, the paper tissue count was significantly lower at four weeks afer treatment.
S713: Injection of both parotid and submandibular glands was reported by Costa and colleagues, who showed a 70% mean reduction in VAS of sialorrhea severity in 12 of 16 participants, and a 60% reduction in cotton roll weight to judge saliva production (Costa 2008).
S714: Porta and colleagues reported improvement in sialorrhea in three of the four participants with MND who had ultrasound-guided botulinum toxin injections in both parotid and submandibular glands (Porta 2001).
S715: In this study, a VAS completed by participants was used to assess the rate of salivation.
S716: In a study by Manrique and colleagues, four of the five participants with MND injected with botulinum toxin in the parotid and submandibular glands reported significant improvement of symptoms and quality of life, based on a clinical questionnaire (Manrique 2005).
S717: Another study reported an overall significant reduction of sialorrhea, as measured by cotton roll weight, and significant improvement in four clinical evaluation scales in a group of 18 participants (nine with MND and nine with Parkinson's disease) who had undergone ultrasound-guided injections of botulinum toxin in the parotid and mandibular glands (Contarino 2007).
S718: A 2011 open-label study of botulinum toxin type A in 10 participants with MND who were refractory to amitriptyline and ipratropium and with a gastrostomy feeding tube showed that manually-guided submandibular injections produced some benefit in 11/12 for up to 12 weeks (Rodriguez-Murphy 2011).
S719: Botulinum toxin injections to the salivary glands are an invasive and relatively high-cost intervention.
S720: They have a number of potential side eﬀects; the more common ones include viscous saliva, local pain, chewing weakness and, because the individual dose-response is unpredictable, dry mouth (Costa 2008; Porta 2001).
S721: Treatment generally appears well tolerated, though serious adverse eﬀects such as infection of salivary gland, dysphagia and recurrent jaw dislocation are rarely reported (Meijer 2008; Tan 2001; Winterholler 2001).
S722: Radiotherapy The published data on radiotherapy of salivary glands to reduce saliva production in MND is all open-label.
S723: Andersen and colleagues reported on a prospective pilot study of low-dose radiation therapy to the parotid and submandibular glands of participants with MND (Andersen 2001).
S724: On each side, the radiation dose was 7 Gy as a single dose in the first 13 participants and 7.5 Gy in the last five participants.
S725: Sixteen of the 18 participants treated reported satisfactory to good reduction in drooling lasting up to four to six months, one participant developed xerostomia (dry mouth) and needed saliva substitutes and another participant reported no eﬀect.
S726: Another study reported treating the submandibular and parotid glands of five MND participants with a single fraction of 8 Gy and four participants with two fractions of 6.25 Gy (total 12.5 Gy) (Harriman 2001).
S727: It was found that 8 Gy of radiation was eﬀective in controlling drooling and increasing the dose did not improve initial control.
S728: In the 8 Gy group, four of the five participants had a significant subjective benefit at two weeks post- treatment.
S729: In this group, three of five participants completed the two-month visit and, of these, two had significant benefit both when reported by participants (measured by a questionnaire) and objectively (measured by the weight of tissue rolls).
S730: In the 12.5 Gy group, one of three participants alive at two weeks post-treatment reported benefit, while both the participants that completed the two-month visit showed improvement on participant-reported and non-participant-reported measures.
S731: Long-term control of sialorrhea was diﬀicult to evaluate as the participants entered in the study were very ill, and their life expectancy was short.

S736: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Stalpers and Moser reported treating 19 participants with MND with radiotherapy to the parotid glands (Stalpers 2002).
S737: The prescribed radiation dose in all participants was 12 Gy in two fractions once a week.
S738: Three participants received one fraction only, of which two already had a satisfactory response afer the first fraction, and one did not want a second fraction.
S739: Fourteen participants reported a satisfactory response within two to three weeks of the initial radiation, complete in 11 and partial in three.
S740: Four participants without improvement afer initial treatment were re-irradiated, with only one showing partial improvement (Stalpers 2002).
S741: Radiotherapy targeted to a single parotid gland, with a total dose of 1500 cGy in three fractions at a depth determined by CT scanning, was trialled in 10 ALS participants with drooling refractory to medical management.
S742: One participant received additional radiotherapy to the contralateral parotid due to persistent secretions.
S743: All participants reported improvement with a reduction in the intensity and amount of drooling (Kasarskis 2011).
S744: In contrast, the case series published by Bourry and colleagues varied the clinical target including the parotids and submandibular glands (18 participants), submandibular glands and one parotid (one participant), or parotids (two participants) (Bourry 2013).
S745: All participants had previously received pharmacological treatments with unsatisfactory results or side eﬀects.
S746: The mean dose was 19.1 Gy (range 3 Gy to 48 Gy), delivered in five fractions (range 1 Gy to 16 Gy) on 17 days (range 1 Gy to 77 Gy).
S747: A positive response was observed in 65% of participants during a mean of seven months (range 1 to 23 Gy).
S748: Positive responses were more common with a high total dose (≥ 16 Gy; 78.6%) than a low total dose (< 16 Gy; 33%; P = 0.07) (Bourry 2013).
S749: Guy and colleagues have published a case series treated with radiotherapy of salivary glands between 2002 and 2007, with diﬀerent treatment protocols according to the decision of their radiotherapist.
S750: Eighty per cent of participants felt improved at one month and 43% at six months afer external radiation (Guy 2011).
S751: Adverse eﬀects of radiotherapy in these studies were mild, such as parotid pain and dry mouth (Andersen 2001).
S752: Bourry and colleagues suggested that photon external beam radiotherapy was more likely to cause adverse eﬀects, as four participants (out of 13) treated with photons and no participants (out of eight) treated with electrons experienced acute toxicity with oral pain (Bourry 2013).
S753: Neppelberg and colleagues reported the eﬀect of single-dose radiotherapy for sialorrhea in people with MND.
S754: They gave radiotherapy to 14 people with MND, consisting of a single fraction of 7.5 Gy to submandibular and parotid glands, repeated for two participants afer six months.
S755: Five participants declined radiotherapy and were given treatment with botulinum injections bilaterally to parotids; two of these apparently later had radiotherapy (Neppelberg 2007).
S756: Surgery There are very few published case series of surgery for sialorrhea.
S757: In one small series, five people with MND having submandibular gland excisions had only limited control of drooling, whereas one having unilateral tympanic neurectomy showed better treatment response (Janzen 1988).
S758: A U T H O R S ' C O N C L U S I O N S Implications for practice There is extremely limited evidence from randomized controlled trials about treatments to reduce sialorrhea in motor neuron disease (MND).
S759: We identified three placebo-controlled studies and one active comparator study for this systematic review.
S760: Botulinum toxin B injected into parotid and submandibular glands may have beneficial eﬀects at eight weeks on number of participants reporting improvement in sialorrhea and probably reduces the volume of saliva produced at eight weeks.
S761: There may be no eﬀect on quality of life.
S762: Oral administration of 20 mg dextromethorphan hydrobromide and 10 mg quinidine sulfate twice daily, the combination termed DMQ, probably slightly improves patient- reported sialorrhea.
S763: The certainty of evidence from a placebo- controlled study that examined scopolamine (hyoscine) patches in a cross-over design and a randomized comparison between botulinum toxin A and radiotherapy were too low for any conclusions to be drawn.
S764: Implications for research There is a need for considerable further work on treating this distressing symptom.
S765: Medications such as those described in the background of this review should be a focus of future randomised controlled trials.
S766: Fundamental understanding of the nature of sialorrhea is required to diﬀerentiate between thick, mucous secretions and watery, salivary drooling.
S767: It would also be useful to further validate a patient self-report measure, including minimal clinically important diﬀerence, and to examine the psychometric characteristics of VAS for bulbar symptoms.
S768: Future research designs must also reflect the need for objective outcome measures such as rate of production of saliva or salivary gland scintigraphy.
S769: These are necessary because an eﬀective intervention may have an unblinding eﬀect.
S770: Placebo-controlled trials with intent-to-treat analysis are still important, as there may be many unpredictable factors which influence sialorrhea, such as mouth breathing, general hydration, diet, disease stage, and concomitant medication.
